1: The turning back to the wilderness toward the Red Sea (penu va-se'u ha-midbar derekh yam-suf) is the forty-year detour reduced to a phrase. The long time in Seir (yemim rabbim) encompasses thirty-eight years of wilderness wandering.
2: YHWH speaks to Moses: the resumption of divine speech after the judgment of ch 1 is the covenant-continuation. YHWH does not permanently withhold his word from Moses despite the people's failure.
3: Rav lakhem sov et ha-har hazeh (you have been going around this mountain long enough). The second rav lakhem (enough for you) of Deuteronomy — the first was at Horeb (1:6), now at Seir. The pattern: YHWH marks the end of each waiting-period and initiates movement.
4: The Esau-people (am Esau) are Israel's brothers (acheikhem) — the Jacob-Esau family relationship is the theological ground for non-aggression toward Edom. The command hishashru me'od (be very careful) adds intensity: the care required is maximal.
5: Lo eten lakhem me'artsam ad midhrak kaf-ragel (I will not give you any of their land — not even a footstep). The specificity of the non-grant is deliberate: just as YHWH has given Seir to Esau as a possession (yerushah), so YHWH has given Israel its inheritance separately. The inheritance-theology of Deuteronomy applies even to non-covenant nations.
6: The purchase of food and water from Edom (kesep tishberu) rather than taking it is the practical application of the non-aggression command. Israel is to be a paying guest through Edomite territory.
7: Ki YHWH Eloheikhah berakhakha b'khol ma'aseh yadekha (YHWH your God has blessed you in all the work of your hands). The blessing in ordinary labor (ma'aseh yadekha — the work of your hands) is the covenant-provision theology: the daily sustenance of the wilderness journey is the outcome of divine blessing, not coincidence. Yada YHWH (YHWH knew/watched over) — the intimate yada (know) applied to YHWH's oversight of their forty-year journey.
8: The passage through Edom (va-na'avor me'et acheinu venei Esav) without conflict is the demonstration of YHWH's ordering of geopolitical relationships. Elath and Ezion-geber are the port cities at the northern end of the Gulf of Aqaba — the route through Edom's territory.
9: The command not to harass Moab (al-tsar et-Mo'av v'al-titgar bam milchamah) mirrors the Edom prohibition. The Lot-connection: uvnei Lot natati et-Ar yerushah (I have given Ar to the sons of Lot as a possession). The descendants of Abraham's nephew receive their territory by divine grant, just as Israel does.
10: The antiquarian note about the Emim (giants, am gadol v'rav v'ram ka-Anakim — a great, numerous, and tall people like the Anakim) explains the pre-existing population of Moabite territory. The editorial notes in ch 2 (vv 10-12, 20-23) are parenthetical historical insertions typical of Deuteronomic narrative style.
11: The Emim were counted as Repha'im (the giant-descended peoples). The Moabites called them Emim; the Israelites recognized them as Rephaim. The terminological note shows the narrator's interest in the prehistory of the nations surrounding Israel.
12: The Horites (ha-Chorim) previously inhabited Seir before Esau's descendants dispossessed them. The comparison to Israel dispossessing Canaan (ka-asher asah Yisra'el le-erets yerushato) provides a typological parallel: just as Esau received his territory through conquest, so will Israel.
13: The crossing of the Zered brook (nachal Zered) marks the end of the Edom/Seir detour and the beginning of the approach to Moab. Qumu v'ivru (rise up and cross) — YHWH's command initiates each movement.
14: The thirty-eight-year duration (shloshim u'shmoneh shanah) from Kadesh-barnea to the Zered crossing is the precise measurement of the judgment-period — the time from the spy-rebellion until the entire military generation died. The note that YHWH swore against them (asher nishba YHWH lahem) connects this to the oath of 1:34-35.
15: Ki af yad-YHWH hayetah bam (for indeed the hand of YHWH was against them). The divine active opposition to the oath-violating generation: YHWH's yad (hand) is elsewhere the instrument of salvation (1:30); here it is the instrument of judgment. The hand that carries (1:31) also strikes when covenant is violated.
16: The all-men-of-war having perished (va-yehi ka-asher tamu kol-anshei ha-milchamah lamut) is the condition that makes the forward movement possible. The judgment was complete; the new generation can now proceed.
17: YHWH speaks to Moses again: the renewed divine speech after the completion of judgment signals the covenant resumption. The pattern of Deuteronomy's narrative is death-judgment followed by YHWH's re-engagement with the surviving community.
18: Attah over et-gevul Mo'av et-Ar (today you are crossing the border of Moab, through Ar). The day-of-crossing precision connects to the calendrical exactness of ch 1 — covenant history is precisely dated.
19: The command not to harass Ammon (al-tsar et-benei Ammon v'al-titgar bam) extends the non-aggression principle to the third Abrahamic-adjacent people. U-venei Lot netatiha yerushah (I have given it as a possession to the sons of Lot) — the same inheritance-theology as v 9.
20: The land of Ammon was also counted as Rephaim-territory (erets Repha'im techashev af hi). The Ammonites called the former inhabitants Zamzummim (another name for the giant-descended Rephaim).
21: The Zamzummim were as great and numerous and tall as the Anakim, but YHWH destroyed them before Ammon and Ammon possessed their territory. The divine-sovereign destruction of pre-existing populations is the theological pattern that anticipates Israel's own conquest.
22: The comparison: as YHWH did for Esau's descendants in Seir (destroying the Horites) and for the Moabites (destroying the Emim), so he will do for Israel. The pattern of YHWH clearing territory for the nations he assigns it to establishes his universal sovereign claim over all land.
23: The Avvim (ha-Avvim) who lived in villages as far as Gaza were destroyed by the Caphtorites (likely the Sea Peoples/Philistines) who came from Caphtor. The note extends the pattern of divinely-governed territorial change beyond Israel's direct experience.
24: Qumu se'u u-ivru et-nachal Arnon (rise up, journey, and cross the Arnon). The Arnon crossing marks the entry into territory that YHWH has given Israel to conquer. Natatati v'yadkha et-Sichon (I have given Sichon into your hand) — the past-tense divine gift of a future conquest: prophetic certainty expressed as already-accomplished.
25: Ha-yom hazeh achel tet pachd'kha v'yir'atka al-penei ha-ammim tachat kol ha-shamayim (this day I will begin to put the dread of you and fear of you on all peoples under all the heavens). The divine spreading of Israel's pachad (dread/terror) and yir'ah (fear) on the nations is the reputation-effect of YHWH's victories. The chalchala (writhing/anguish) of the nations at the report of Israel is the physical symptom of this divinely-induced terror.
26: Moses sent messengers (mal'akhim) from the wilderness of Kedemoth with words of peace (divrei shalom) to Sichon. Even to the enemy Israel must offer peace before engaging in battle — the Deuteronomic war law (20:10-12) is applied before it is formally stated.
27: The request to pass through on the king's highway (ba-derekh ba-derekh elek — on the road, on the road I will travel) without turning right or left (lo asur yamin u'smol) is the standard non-aggression travel-request. The same request was made to Edom (Num 20:17-18).
28: The offer to buy food and water (okhel tishbor li b'khesep) mirrors the Edom arrangement (v 6). Israel offers full commercial exchange, not conquest.
29: The reference to the sons of Esau and Moab having allowed Israel to pass through (ka-asher asu li venei Esav ha-yoshevim b'Se'ir v'ha-Mo'avim ha-yoshevim b'Ar) establishes the precedent. Until crossing the Jordan, Israel's policy is peaceful transit.
30: Va-lo avah Sichon melekh Cheshbon ha'avireinu bo (but Sichon king of Heshbon was not willing to let us pass through him). Ki hiqshah YHWH Eloheikhah et-rucho v'imets et-levavo (for YHWH your God hardened his spirit and made his heart courageous/stubborn). The hardening of Sichon's spirit by YHWH is the Pharaoh-pattern (Exod 4:21, 7:3) applied to the Amorite king: YHWH uses Sichon's refusal to create the occasion for Israel's victory and the beginning of conquest.
31: Ra'eh hachiloti tet lefaneikha et-Sichon (see, I have begun to give Sichon before you). The hachiloti (I have begun) marks the incipient conquest — the first of many such divine deliveries. Hachel rash le-rish et-artso (begin to take possession of his land) — the divine initiative empowers the human action.
32: Sichon came out against Israel at Jahaz (va-yetse Sichon liqratenu hu v'khal-ammo) — the initiative for battle was Sichon's. Israel did not attack without provocation; Sichon refused passage and then attacked. The va-yitten YHWH Eloheinu otho lefaneinu (YHWH our God gave him into our hand) is the covenant-victory formula.
33: Israel struck him and his sons and all his people (va-nakh otho v'et-banav v'et-khal-ammo). The cherem-logic (though the term is not used here explicitly) applies: total defeat of the enemy as YHWH's military-holy act.
34: Va-nilkhod et-khal-arav ba-et ha-hi (we captured all his cities at that time). The ba-et ha-hi (at that time) anchors the summary in the specific historical moment. The nishim v'taf (men, women, and children) all under the ban (nakhrim) — the total-devotion formula of holy war.
35: Only the livestock and plunder of the captured cities were kept by Israel — the standard exception to the ban: animals and goods are taken as spoil; persons are not.
36: From Aroer on the Arnon to Gilead: no city was too strong (lo hayetah qiryah asher sagvah mimmennu) for YHWH's giving. The comprehensive character of the victory is attributed entirely to YHWH's sovereign gift.
37: The exception: raq el-erets benei Ammon lo qaravta (but to the land of the Ammonites you did not go near). The specific prohibition against Ammonite territory (v 19) was observed: YHWH's non-conquest-zones were honored. This demonstrates that Israel's obedience in the Transjordan was genuine — they took exactly what YHWH gave and left what YHWH excluded.
